original_abstract:
Radical cross-coupling reactions have transformed synthetic chemistry, providing powerful strategies for assembling C(sp3)–C and C(sp3)–heteroatom bonds through transition-metal catalysis, often in tandem with photoredox or electrochemical methods. In contrast, utilizing the unique properties of main-group elements to drive such radical pathways remains significantly underdeveloped. 

In this work, we demonstrate that a low-valent bismuthinidene complex can undergo one-electron oxidative addition with redox-active alkyl electrophiles, emulating the radical reactivity typically reserved for first-row transition metals. This approach provides access to a series of well-defined alkyl–bismuth(III) oxidative addition complexes, which we have fully characterized in both solution and the solid state. We found that these intermediates exhibit divergent reactivity governed by the α-substituents of the alkyl fragment: while unbiased alkyl–bismuth species are relatively stable, α-amino derivatives readily evolve into iminium ions. Leveraging these mechanistic insights, we developed a bismuth-catalyzed C(sp3)–N cross-coupling reaction. This transformation proceeds under mild conditions without the need for external photocatalysts or oxidants, enabling the efficient functionalization of synthetically and biologically relevant NH-heterocycles.
humanized_abstract:
The vast potential of the classic radical cross-coupling reactions and their impact on modern synthetic chemistry, especially through transition metal-catalyzed C(sp3)–C and C(sp3)–heteroatom bond formation, achieved either directly or in conjunction with photoredox or even electrochemical activation, is amply demonstrated. Conversely, the as yet untapped potential of main-group elements for activating main-group-element-based homogeneous radical cross-coupling reactions is still largely unexploited.

Low-valent bismuthinidene reacts with redox-active alkyl-electrophiles to give alkyl-bismuth(III) species via a one-electron oxidative addition mechanism that mimics the radical reactivity characteristic of early transition metals. These alkyl–bismuth(III) species have been fully characterised in solution and solid state and behave depending on their α-substituent: neutral alkyl–bismuth adducts are rather stable but σ-amino derivatives lead to the formation of iminium species. Using these species as intermediates we designed and optimised a bismuth catalysed C(sp3)–N cross coupling between inexpensive substrates and NH-heterocycles of high relevance in both synthetic and biological fields, all in catalytic mode and under very mild conditions (room temperature, without any photo-catalyst and with just air as oxidant).

Conversely, the as yet untapped potential of main-group elements for activating main-group-element-based homogeneous radical cross-coupling reactions is still largely unexploited.

Low-valent bismuthinidene reacts with redox-active alkyl-electrophiles to give alkyl-bismuth(III) species via a one-electron oxidative addition mechanism that mimics the radical reactivity characteristic of early transition metals. These alkyl–bismuth(III) species have been fully characterised in solution and solid state and behave depending on their α-substituent: neutral alkyl–bismuth adducts are rather stable but σ-amino derivatives lead to the formation of iminium species.
